clinical_coherence.notes: 42F South Asian with BCG vaccination history, travel to TB-endemic India, family TB history, presenting with subacute (2-week) meningitis. Basilar meningitis pattern, cranial nerve VI and VII palsies, SIADH (Na 128), weight loss, and night sweats form a perfect clinical picture of tuberculous meningitis. The coherence between demographics, risk factors, and presentation is outstanding.

tool_output_consistency.notes: CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (220 cells, 70% lymphocytes), very high protein (185), very low glucose (22, ratio 0.22), positive AFB smear, positive GeneXpert. MRI shows basilar meningeal enhancement with tuberculomas and hydrocephalus. Labs show SIADH, positive QuantiFERON, low procalcitonin. All tool outputs are perfectly concordant for TBM.

ground_truth_quality.notes: Primary diagnosis is TBM (ICD A17.0), which is correct. However, this case is filed under 'bacterial_meningitis' condition, which is technically accurate (TB is bacterial) but may create confusion as TBM has distinct management (RIPE + dexamethasone vs standard empiric antibiotics). Critical actions are clinically excellent: RIPE, dexamethasone, hydrocephalus monitoring. Red herrings are well-constructed.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Outstanding diagnostic puzzle. The subacute course, lymphocytic CSF, basilar pattern, low procalcitonin, and epidemiologic clues all require the clinician to differentiate TBM from pyogenic bacterial meningitis. BCG vaccination and absent pulmonary symptoms are intentional confounders.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 42F Indian-born US resident with travel to rural Maharashtra, family TB history, and BCG vaccination is a perfectly realistic demographic for TBM in the developed world.

red_herring_quality.notes: Three excellent red herrings: BCG vaccination (falsely reassuring), low procalcitonin (may suggest against infection), and absent pulmonary symptoms (may lead away from TB). Each is clinically realistic and educationally valuable with clear correct interpretations.

educational_value.notes: Exceptional teaching case covering TBM diagnosis, the CSF glucose ratio significance, basilar meningitis pattern, SIADH in TBM, adjunctive dexamethasone benefit, and multiple clinical pearls. The follow-up outputs (AFB culture, chest CT, ophthalmology with choroidal tubercles) are outstanding.

strengths: Masterfully constructed TBM case with excellent clinical detail
Red herrings (BCG, low procalcitonin, no cough) are clever and realistic
Follow-up outputs (choroidal tubercles, chest CT, repeat LP) add exceptional depth
The SIADH, cranial nerve palsies, and hydrocephalus form a complete TBM syndrome
Outstanding educational value for distinguishing TBM from pyogenic meningitis
